text: We present a robotic radical prostatectomy in its entirety. There are minimal edits for non-operative portions of the case. This is a fifty-six year old male with a PSA of five and a digital rectal exam showing a small nodule. He is in good health with no prior family history. He has prior surgery with laparoscopic bilateral inguinal hernia repair with mesh. His biopsy showed a glisten score of three plus four disease. After discussing options, he is elected for robotic prostatectomy.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to lyse adhesions in the pelvic space, carefully navigating around the mesh from previous laparoscopic hernia repair. The mesh is covered with peritoneum and some adhering viscera, requiring precise handling to avoid damaging the fibrotic tissue in the space of Retzius.

The robotic setup for the prostatectomy involves a five-port technique, with three robotic arms and a camera controlled by the robot. The assistant uses a twelve millimeter port to apply suction, traction, and clips during the procedure.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to clear the peritoneal working space, minimizing the use of cautery to reduce the risk of bowel injury during the robotic prostatectomy.

The surgeon uses robotic monopolar scissors in the right hand and bipolar forceps in the left hand, while the third arm, the ProGraph, provides manipulation and counter-traction of tissues during the robotic prostatectomy.

The surgeon begins the posterior dissection using a transperitoneal approach, focusing on the precise dissection of the seminal vesicles and vasa. The seminal vesicles are visible with their associated arteries, which are clipped to minimize cautery impact on surrounding tissues, particularly lateral nerves involved in sexual function.

The surgeon uses curable dissection lateral to the seminal vesicles, aiming to spare neurovascular tissue to maximize the patient's chance of recovery. A nerve monitor is employed to measure signals from the neurovascular bundles, assessing their location and preservation.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the seminal vesicles and proceeds to dissect Denonvilliers' fascia, a membranous partition separating the prostate and urinary bladder from the rectum. Cold dissection with sharp scissors is used to minimize the risk of rectal injury.

The surgeon uses the left bipolar instrument to pull on the medial umbilical ligament while dissecting laterally with hot scissors. The previously placed mesh is visible and close to the dissection area, requiring careful navigation through the areolar tissue to locate the pubic symphysis and avoid damage to surrounding vasculature.

The surgeon mobilizes the anterior bladder wall and proceeds to identify the endophilic fascia. The periprostatic fat surrounding the prostate is carefully removed, minimizing blood loss and improving tissue visibility for subsequent dissection.

The robotic-assisted dissection is performed along the levator muscles near the dorsal vein, sparing individual muscle fibers. Cold dissection is used to minimize damage to muscles and nerve fibers. The surgeon is on the left side of the prostate, cutting across the puboprostatic ligaments to expose the dorsal vein.

The surgeon uses robotic needle drivers to suture the dorsal vein with a zero PDS suture, demonstrating a slip knot technique to ensure a tight seal and minimize bleeding. The suture is placed carefully to avoid interference with nerve bundles that may run high on the prostate.

The surgeon performs an anterior urethra pexy as part of the reconstruction, positioning the urethra anteriorly to restore continence. The prostate has been removed, and the urethral angle is adjusted. The Propep electrodes are introduced for nerve monitoring, preparing for the upcoming bladder neck dissection.

The ProPep system is integrated into the surgical robot. When we are ready to stimulate, we simply press a button and instead of bipolar current, we send a signal which stimulates the nerves that run around the prostate. The signals are picked up by the electrodes and we see a characteristic waveform which lets us know that nerves that may be involved with continence and possibly sexual function are nearby. We can thus tailor the surgery individually to a patient. Also, at the end of the case, we can obtain a final stimulation and see the characteristics of the nerve signals at this point. And we can tell that the nerves are similar to how we had started at the beginning of the case, which helps us give quantitative information to the patient.

The surgeon performs dissection of the bladder neck, using a full Foley balloon to bounce back and forth, aiding in identifying the interface between the prostate and bladder despite unclear surgical planes.

The surgeon carefully opens the bladder neck, minimizing traction to prevent enlargement and potential leakage during the subsequent anastomosis between the bladder and urethra. The dissection proceeds through an areolar tissue plane, approaching the vas deferens and seminal vesicles, while minimizing the risk of injury to the posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon reveals the shiny white surface of the seminal vesicles and vasa, indicating the completion of the posterior bladder neck dissection. Attention is now directed towards the prostate pedicles, where nerve-sparing techniques are initiated. An anti-grade and retrograde release of the neurovascular bundles is performed to minimize tension and potential neuropraxia.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the neurovascular bundles using a combination of sharp, blunt, and pinpoint cautery techniques, ensuring to stay high above the nerve bundles for optimal nerve tissue release. The apex of the prostate is visible, along with the site of the prior dorsal vein stitch. Cold irrigation is applied to minimize heat damage during cautery use.

The surgeon completes the release of the neurovascular bundles and proceeds with posterior dissection in the area of the previously opened non-VAS fascia. Using a sweeping motion, the perirectal tissue and neurovascular bundles are moved away from the prostate.

The surgeon applies a hemolog clip to the vascular pedicle after safely removing the neurovascular bundles. Cautery or additional clips are used to secure the proximal part of the pedicle, reducing back bleeding and keeping the surgical field dry. Pinpoint cautery and small clips are utilized to seal veins and small arteries during the nerve dissection, with copious irrigation minimizing heat transfer.

The surgeon performs dissection on the right side, releasing the nerve bundles in both antegrade and retrograde fashion. The right posterior pedicle is dissected using the same principles, with careful manipulation to preserve nerve integrity.

The surgeon completes the apical dissection using cautery and sharp dissection near the region of the previously applied dorsal vein suture. Copious irrigation is used to maintain a clear surgical field, ensuring the vascular bundles remain distant from the dissection area. The surgeon pinches the tissue to differentiate it from the prostate.

The surgeon enters the space where the urethra lies, exposing areolar tissue. Dissection of the urethra is performed with careful preservation of urethral length to avoid positive margins. Visualization is clear, with minimal bleeding, and the assistant irrigates the field to distinguish prostate tissue from urethra. Cautery is used as the neurovascular bundles are safely out of the operative field.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis in a running fashion, minimizing tissue handling and reducing the risk of postoperative leakage.

The surgeon completes the final portion of the anastomosis and tests the Foley catheter by injecting 120 cc of sterile saline to check for leaks. This ensures the integrity of the anastomosis and reduces the risk of urethral strictures, which can manifest as bladder neck contractures.

The surgeon completes the procedure by dropping the pneumoperitoneum to check for any bleeding. The pouch of Douglas is inspected for bowel injuries and fluid collections, ensuring no unnoticed complications during the robotic radical prostatectomy.
